good: I'm a final-year Computer Science student with a strong interest in backend development. I've built two full-stack projects — a hostel management system and a real-time chat app — and interned briefly with a startup where I worked on REST APIs. I'm looking to join a team where I can keep growing as a software engineer and contribute to real-world products.
bad: My name is Rahul, I am from Salem, I did my schooling there, then I joined this college for B.Tech CSE, and now I am in final year. I like coding and I am a hardworking and honest person.

good: I bring a solid foundation in data structures and problem-solving, hands-on project experience shipping working software end-to-end, and I learn new tools quickly — I picked up Docker and basic CI/CD on my own for my last project. I'm confident I can start contributing within my first few weeks.
bad: Because I really need this job and I have studied a lot for it. I promise I will work very hard if you give me a chance.

good: I used to over-engineer solutions before writing any code, spending too long designing instead of building. I've fixed this by timeboxing my design phase and validating with a quick prototype first, which has made me noticeably faster without losing quality.
bad: I don't really have any weaknesses, I am a perfectionist and I work too hard sometimes.

q: Where do you see yourself in 5 years?
good: I'd like to grow from writing individual features to owning a full module or service, and eventually mentor newer engineers. I'm also interested in going deeper into system design, so I see myself taking on more architecture-level responsibility over time.
bad: I want to become a manager and earn a lot of money. Maybe I will do MBA also, not sure.

q: Describe a time you faced a conflict in a team and how you resolved it.
good: During a hackathon, a teammate and I disagreed on using SQL vs NoSQL for our project. Instead of arguing, we listed our actual query patterns, timeboxed a 20-minute comparison, and picked SQL because our data was relational. We shipped on time and both felt heard.
bad: There was a fight in my group but I just stayed quiet and let the team leader decide because I don't like conflicts.

q: Why do you want to work for our company?
good: I've used your product and looked into your engineering blog — I liked how your team approaches scaling your search infrastructure. I want to work somewhere I can learn from senior engineers on hard technical problems, and your emphasis on code quality and mentorship stood out to me.
bad: Your company is very famous and pays well, and it will look good on my resume.

q: How do you handle failure or a project that didn't go as planned?
good: In my second-year project, our deployed app crashed under a small load test right before demo day. I stayed calm, isolated the bottleneck to an unindexed query, fixed it, and we demoed successfully an hour late. I now always load-test before deadlines.
bad: I get very upset when I fail but then after some days I feel okay and try again.

q: Do you have any questions for us?
good: Yes — could you tell me more about what a typical onboarding process looks like for new graduate hires, and what does success look like in the first six months on this team?
bad: No, I think you explained everything already.
